programs.program_name: PhD in Systems, Synthetic, and Quantitative Biology

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT min 95 or IELTS Academic 7.0; exempt if undergraduate degree was earned where English is the primary language of instruction (a master's does not count).
requirements.admission_model_note: Committee-based admission through Harvard Integrated Life Sciences (HILS); students are not admitted to a specific lab. First-year students complete 3-4 lab rotations before formally joining a thesis lab.

funding.note: All admitted students who enroll, including international students, receive guaranteed support (tuition, health insurance, and a competitive stipend) for the program's duration. No specific stipend figure is published on the official page.

requirements.contact_note: Program states that contacting professors before applying is not required and does not increase admission chances.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Galit Lahav
faculty.title: Novartis Professor of Systems Biology; Chair, Department of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Lahav uses quantitative live single-cell imaging, fluorescent reporters, and mathematical modeling to study how the temporal dynamics of signaling proteins, especially the tumor suppressor p53, control cell-fate decisions after DNA damage. Recent work decoded p53 oscillations with time-series omics, showed p53/p21 dynamics encode single-cell damage levels to tune proliferation and heterogeneity, examined entrainment and multistability of the p53 oscillator, and found cell confluency reshapes p53 dynamics. Her lab also studies ERK dynamics in oncogene-induced proliferation.

faculty.name: Allon Klein
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Klein develops single-cell genomic technologies, notably the inDrops droplet scRNA-seq platform, alongside computational and theoretical methods to understand how cells choose fates in development and regeneration. He pioneered clonal lineage tracing with LARRY lentiviral barcoding, linking transcriptional state to future fate. Recent directions include developmental cell atlases, cross-species comparison and the evolution of cell types, inferring dynamics and fate probabilities from single-cell snapshots, and germ-layer specification and stochastic fate decisions in Xenopus and mammalian systems.

faculty.name: Debora Marks
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Marks builds machine-learning and statistical methods that learn from evolutionary sequence variation to predict protein structure, function, and the effects of genetic variants. Recent work includes EVE, a deep generative model predicting variant pathogenicity, protein language models such as Tranception, and ProteinGym, large-scale benchmarks for variant-effect and fitness prediction. She applies these approaches to antibody and protein design and to the clinical interpretation of variants of unknown significance, bridging computational biology and human genetics.

faculty.name: Johan Paulsson
faculty.title: Arthur K. Solomon Professor of Systems Biology in the Field of Biophysics
faculty.summary: Paulsson studies how randomness arising from small molecule numbers, or noise, governs single-cell behavior in bacteria and yeast. His lab develops high-throughput, long-term mother-machine microfluidics and single-cell movie cytometry, plus the SIFT method to track and recover individual cells across generations. Applications span replication control, molecule partitioning at cell division, stress responses, toxin-antitoxin systems, and stochastic proteolysis. He also leads a large federally funded program targeting antibiotic resistance through quantitative single-cell approaches.

faculty.name: Peter Sorger
faculty.title: Otto Krayer Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Sorger builds combined experimental and computational models of signal-transduction and cell-death networks to explain cancer drug response and resistance. A major recent thrust is highly multiplexed tissue imaging by cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF and t-CyCIF), extended to high-plex 3D confocal imaging of thick tissue. As co-director of the Harvard Tissue Atlas, his group mapped the spatial landscape of primary melanoma progression and immunoediting at single-cell resolution and profiled immune-related adverse events from checkpoint-blockade therapy.

faculty.name: Vamsi Mootha
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology; HHMI Investigator
faculty.summary: Mootha combines genomics, computation, and biochemistry to study mitochondrial function in health and disease. A central recent theme is oxygen as therapy: chronic hypoxia reversed disease in a Leigh syndrome mouse model and improved Friedreich's ataxia models, and 2024 work advanced therapeutic hypoxia via enhanced hemoglobin oxygen affinity and HIF-2-alpha inhibition. Mechanistic studies showed hypoxia and intragenic suppressors rescue Complex I mutants through a shared mechanism. He maintains MitoCarta, the reference atlas of mitochondrial proteins.

faculty.name: Pamela Silver
faculty.title: Elliot T. and Onie H. Adams Professor of Biochemistry and Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Silver pursues synthetic and systems biology for health and sustainability. Recent work engineers gut bacteria that persist long-term as living diagnostics sensing intestinal inflammation, and builds whole-cell biosensors. On sustainability, her lab re-engineers the cyanobacterial carboxysome to boost carbon fixation, develops bionic-leaf systems coupling solar water-splitting to microbial bioproduction, and engineers bacteria for carbon capture and mineral weathering. She is also a core faculty member of the Wyss Institute, translating engineered biological systems toward real-world applications.

faculty.name: Peng Yin
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Yin programs DNA and RNA as engineerable materials for imaging and measurement. Recent efforts center on DNA-PAINT super-resolution microscopy, enabling highly multiplexed, quantitative imaging below five nanometers and extending it deeper into cells. A major thrust develops single-molecule protein identification and sequencing by barcoding and stretching proteins, enabling single-cell proteomics and improved biomarker detection for cancer and neurodegeneration. His Molecular Systems Lab also builds programmable self-assembling nucleic-acid structures and devices.

faculty.name: Markus Basan
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Basan combines quantitative experiments and theory to uncover principles of microbial physiology. Recent papers dissect metabolic strategies during growth and adaptation, showing that glycolysis and gluconeogenesis specialization is driven by biochemical constraints of flux sensing. Other work analyzes proteome adaptation, revealing the bacterial envelope's role in starvation survival, and shows that starving-bacteria survival dynamics are set by ion homeostasis maintaining plasmolysis. Broader themes include microbial growth laws, evolutionary tradeoffs, and homeostasis of cell size and composition.

faculty.name: Sahand Hormoz
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology
faculty.summary: Hormoz develops experimental and computational tools to understand how cell states and lineages emerge. Recent work reconstructs lineage histories and differentiation trajectories of individual cancer cells in myeloproliferative neoplasms and deciphers the states and genealogies of human hematopoiesis using naturally occurring somatic mutations. He created methods for de novo somatic-mutation detection in single-cell data, probe-based bacterial scRNA-seq, and differential-geometry approaches for single-cell analysis, and builds organoid models of human somite development.

faculty.name: Michael M. Desai
faculty.title: Professor of Organismic and Evolutionary Biology and of Physics, Harvard University
faculty.summary: Desai combines theory and high-throughput experiments to study how genetic variation arises and how natural selection shapes evolving populations, focusing on the dynamics of asexual microbes and viruses. Recent work uses barcoded yeast lineages and large fitness assays to dissect epistasis, clonal interference, and the predictability of adaptation, connecting population-genetic models to genome-scale data. His group also studies how genotype maps to phenotype across evolutionary trajectories and mentors SSQB students through lab rotations.

faculty.name: Andrew W. Murray
faculty.title: Herchel Smith Professor of Molecular Genetics and Director, FAS Center for Systems Biology, Harvard University
faculty.summary: Murray uses budding yeast as a tractable system to uncover general principles of how cells transmit information during division and mating and how populations evolve under selection. His lab experimentally evolves novel traits such as multicellularity and simple clocks, engineers biological behaviors to test mechanistic understanding, and dissects processes like cell polarization, energy homeostasis, and cell-cycle regulation. Combining genetics, quantitative imaging, and modeling, the group links molecular circuitry to evolutionary and cell-biological outcomes across the tree of life.

faculty.name: Sharad Ramanathan
faculty.title: Llura and Gordon Gund Professor of Neurosciences and of Molecular and Cellular Biology, Harvard University
faculty.summary: Ramanathan studies how multipotent human cells receive and process signals to build correctly patterned tissues of appropriate size, and how nervous systems convert environmental input into behavior. His lab combines stem-cell and molecular biology, genomics, bioengineering, and computational and dynamical-systems theory to map the low-dimensional structure underlying fate decisions in human pluripotent stem cells and to reconstruct signaling geometries that guide morphogenesis. Parallel work in C. elegans identifies key network nodes controlling behavior through direct neural manipulation.

faculty.name: Angela H. DePace
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: DePace investigates how regulatory DNA encodes transcriptional network behavior and ultimately organismal phenotype and evolution. Her lab quantifies how enhancers direct gene expression in space and time, probing the extent to which they act as independent modules, and develops experimental and theoretical approaches to the dynamic, non-equilibrium roles of transcription factors, nucleosome remodeling, and post-translational modification during the transcription cycle. Using Drosophila and quantitative imaging, the group links enhancer logic to network evolution and organismal diversity.

faculty.name: Michael Springer
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: Springer studies how evolution shapes and constrains the way organisms sense and respond to their environment, using related yeast species and combining fluorescence measurements, synthetic and genetic perturbations, and modeling. His group dissects how biochemistry and network wiring let cells process environmental information and choose appropriate responses, mapping genotype to phenotype. Recently he has focused on developing new low-cost diagnostics and on microbial engineering for sustainable production, co-founding the HIVE synthetic-biology institute at HMS.

faculty.name: Sean G. Megason
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: Megason applies imaging-based systems biology to reveal how animal development achieves patterning, morphogenesis, and size control. His lab pioneered in toto imaging, using multicolor transgenic zebrafish and high-resolution timelapse confocal and two-photon microscopy to generate enormous image sets, then tracking every cell with custom software to quantify molecular, cellular, and tissue-scale dynamics. Working in the developing inner ear and spinal cord, the group integrates genetics, quantitative microscopy, and modeling to build predictive, systems-level models of embryogenesis.

faculty.name: Ethan C. Garner
faculty.title: Professor of Molecular and Cellular Biology, Harvard University
faculty.summary: Garner dissects the self-organizing mechanisms that create and read out cell shape in bacteria and archaea, with the aim of uncovering new antibiotic targets. His lab tracks the in vivo dynamics of single molecules using quantitative microscopy combined with genetics and cell biology, studying how cytoskeletal enzymes are spatially regulated so that cells grow as rods of defined width or divide precisely in half. Recent work in Bacillus subtilis links molecular motion to long-range spatial control of growth and division.

faculty.name: Marc W. Kirschner
faculty.title: John Franklin Enders University Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: A founder of the HMS Department of Systems Biology, Kirschner studies fundamental questions of biological organization in space and time. His lab combines biochemical, cell-biological, and embryological approaches to understand cell morphogenesis, proliferation, and signaling, and explores vertebrate evolution through hemichordate development and genomics. Current efforts center on the control of cell and organ size, the Hippo pathway, cell motility, and Wnt signaling, integrating quantitative measurement with mechanistic dissection of how cells count and coordinate their growth.

faculty.name: Timothy J. Mitchison
faculty.title: Hasib Sabbagh Professor of Systems Biology and Co-Director, SSQB PhD Program, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: Mitchison uses systematic, quantitative methods to study basic cell biology and its medical implications, focusing on cytoskeletal structure, dynamics, and function. His lab probes how cell division responds to metabolism, size, shape, and drugs in normal and diseased cells, with a major program on mitotic spindle assembly in Xenopus egg extracts. Combining imaging, biochemistry, pharmacology, and theory, the group connects molecular mechanisms of microtubule dynamics to cell-scale behavior. He co-directs Harvard's SSQB graduate program.

faculty.name: Michael P. Brenner
faculty.title: Michael F. Cronin Professor of Applied Mathematics and Applied Physics and Professor of Physics, Harvard University
faculty.summary: Brenner applies ideas from applied mathematics, physics, and machine learning to foundational and practical problems across science and biology. His group studies self-assembly, asking how simple components with programmable interactions reliably organize into complex structures, and works on soft matter, materials, and fluid mechanics. Recent biological directions link evolution and physiology in voltage-gated ion channels and hemoglobin, transport through the nuclear pore, and methods for analyzing the very large systems of differential equations that arise in biological modeling.

faculty.name: Walter Fontana
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: Fontana develops mathematical and computational frameworks for representing and reasoning about complex molecular systems. He created Kappa, a rule-based modeling language with graph-rewriting semantics that treats signaling like a symbolic chemistry adapted to molecular biology, enabling simulation and causal analysis of large combinatorial signaling networks. His recent work advances counterfactual resimulation for causal inference in rule-based models and knowledge-representation approaches, helping bridge molecular detail and system-level behavior in cellular signal transduction and beyond.

faculty.name: Jennifer Oyler-Yaniv
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Systems Biology, Harvard Medical School
faculty.summary: Oyler-Yaniv leads a lab at the interface of immunology and systems biology, asking quantitative questions about how immune cells communicate. Using cancer and infection as model systems, her group studies how cytokines travel through tissues and how target cells interpret them, focusing on how cytokines drive the self-organization of immune cell niches. By dissecting the molecular, cellular, and tissue-level factors that control spatial cytokine propagation, the work aims to rationally localize cytokines and design more targeted interventions for inflammatory disease.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT required for non-native English speakers; exempt if a prior degree was completed where English is the language of instruction (or with 2+ years of recent English-medium education/work).
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants are admitted to the department, not a specific lab, and need not secure a PI beforehand. Each student receives an initial faculty adviser, rotates through up to three or four labs during year one, and confirms a dissertation advisor by the end of the fourth quarter.

funding.note: Stanford Engineering guarantees full funding (tuition, living stipend, health-insurance subsidy) for PhD students in good standing for the duration of the program, via research assistantships, fellowships, and a required teaching-assistant contribution.

requirements.contact_note: Applicants need not secure a research supervisor before applying; admission is committee-based with first-year rotations.

faculty.name: Karl Deisseroth
faculty.title: D. H. Chen Professor of Bioengineering and of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences; HHMI Investigator
faculty.summary: Deisseroth invents and applies tools to observe and control defined neural cell types in behaving animals, including foundational optogenetics, CLARITY and successor hydrogel-tissue-chemistry methods, and structure-function probes. Recent work maps circuits underlying adaptive and maladaptive behaviors, spanning affective disorders, autism-spectrum conditions, dissociation, thirst, and social behavior, by integrating single-cell-resolution recording, whole-brain clearing and imaging, and clinical translation toward neural-stimulation therapies. His contributions have been recognized with the Lasker and Japan Prizes.

faculty.name: Stanley Qi
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Qi pioneered nuclease-dead Cas9 (dCas9) for programmable gene regulation and has greatly expanded the CRISPR toolbox, including CRISPRi and CRISPRa, hyperCas12a, and the compact CasMINI. Recent work develops precision epigenetic editing in primary and immune cells, large-scale genetic-perturbation screens, RNA and spatial-biology tools, and CRISPR-based platforms for gene and cell therapy targeting immunology and neurobiology. His lab increasingly integrates automation and machine learning into gene-editing experiment design.

faculty.name: Jennifer Brophy
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Brophy builds synthetic genetic circuits to reprogram plant development and to engineer plants together with their associated microbes. Recent work creates libraries of synthetic promoters and logic gates that control gene expression with cell-type and spatial precision in plant roots, aiming to tune root architecture and environmental-stress resilience. The overarching goal is a toolkit that lets engineers program plants for climate adaptation and sustainable agriculture, bridging synthetic biology and plant science.

faculty.name: Markus Covert
faculty.title: Shriram Chair and Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Covert builds computational models of complex cellular processes and uses them to drive experiments. His lab is known for genome-scale whole-cell models, first of Mycoplasma genitalium and now E. coli, that predict phenotype from genotype by integrating metabolic, regulatory, and signaling networks. Recent efforts combine large-scale live-cell imaging and machine learning to study dynamic single-cell signaling, notably NF-kB dynamics in cancer and chemotherapy resistance, and work toward whole-cell models of human cells. He chairs the department.

faculty.name: Polly Fordyce
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering and of Genetics
faculty.summary: Fordyce develops microfluidic platforms for quantitative, high-throughput biophysics, biochemistry, and single-cell genomics. Recent platforms include HT-MEK for high-throughput enzyme kinetics, MITOMI-based binding assays, MRBLEs spectrally encoded beads, and Dropception double-emulsion droplets, which together measure thousands of kinetic and thermodynamic constants at once. Her lab uses these tools to dissect enzyme catalysis, transcription-factor binding energetics, and protein-protein interactions, linking sequence to biophysical function at unprecedented scale. She directs the Stanford Microfluidics Foundry.

faculty.name: Jennifer Cochran
faculty.title: Addie and Al Macovski Professor; Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Cochran uses protein engineering, directed evolution, and biophysics to design molecules for oncology, immunology, and regenerative medicine. Recent work engineers designer protein inputs and synthetic biochemical circuits for cell therapies, multi-specific and receptor-targeting proteins, and tuned growth factors, alongside new high-throughput tools for protein discovery and characterization. Several designs have advanced toward translational and clinical applications. She also holds a senior university leadership role, which may affect student availability.

faculty.name: Scott Delp
faculty.title: James H. Clark Professor; Professor of Bioengineering and of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Delp combines experimental and computational biomechanics to understand human movement, muscle function, and mobility disorders. His lab created OpenSim, the widely used open-source musculoskeletal-simulation platform, and applies it to surgical planning, rehabilitation, and device design. Recent work integrates wearable sensors, video-based motion capture, and machine learning for large-scale, real-world movement analysis, with initiatives such as the Wu Tsai Human Performance Alliance targeting human performance and mobility health. He was the founding chair of the Bioengineering Department.

faculty.name: Manu Prakash
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Prakash studies how physics and computation are embodied in living matter, from single-cell behavior and morphological computing in organisms without neurons to collective multicellular dynamics. His lab invents ultra-low-cost scientific tools, including the Foldscope paper microscope and the Paperfuge, to democratize science and enable field diagnostics for malaria and tuberculosis. Recent work probes marine plankton biophysics, gravity and pressure sensing, and open-hardware instrumentation for planetary-scale biology and global citizen science.

faculty.name: Mark Skylar-Scott
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Skylar-Scott engineers the biomanufacturing of organ-specific tissues at therapeutic scale. His lab develops multimaterial 3D bioprinting and organ-building-block approaches that assemble stem-cell-derived organoids into dense, functional tissues, plus sacrificial-writing (SWIFT) methods to embed perfusable vasculature. Recent projects target vascularized cardiac and kidney tissues and scalable, patient- and organ-specific constructs aimed at regenerative therapies and disease modeling. The work bridges developmental biology, stem-cell science, and advanced manufacturing.

faculty.name: Kwabena Boahen
faculty.title: Professor of Bioengineering and of Electrical Engineering
faculty.summary: Boahen reverse-engineers neural design principles and builds neuromorphic electronic systems that scale energy use with size as efficiently as the brain. His lab created Neurogrid, a platform emulating cortex in biophysical detail, and continues to develop brain-inspired, ultra-low-power computing architectures. Recent work formalizes how dendritic and axonal computation can be mapped to silicon, informing next-generation AI hardware, commercialized through the startup Femtosense. He directs the Brains in Silicon lab.

faculty.name: Russ B. Altman
faculty.title: Kenneth Fong Professor; Professor of Bioengineering, Genetics, and Medicine
faculty.summary: Altman applies artificial intelligence, data science, and informatics to biomedical problems, with a focus on how human genetic variation shapes drug response. His lab develops computational methods spanning molecular, cellular, and population scales, and he helped build Stanford's biomedical informatics and PharmGKB pharmacogenomics resources. A physician-scientist and former Bioengineering chair, he studies drug action, repurposing, and adverse events. His group integrates structural biology, machine learning, and clinical data to guide precision medicine and predict how patients metabolize therapeutics.

faculty.name: Christina D. Smolke
faculty.title: Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Smolke is a pioneer of synthetic biology and metabolic engineering who reprograms cells to manufacture complex plant-derived medicines. Her team engineered baker's yeast to biosynthesize opioids and other benzylisoquinoline alkaloids, compressing multi-step plant pathways into fermentable microbes. She also created RNA-based control devices, or RNA switches, that sense chemical signals and regulate gene expression, providing programmable platforms for biosensing and cellular control. Beyond the lab she co-founded Antheia to scale sustainable pharmaceutical production, and she leads bioengineering education as associate chair.

faculty.name: Jan T. Liphardt
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Liphardt runs a lab of physicists, engineers, and biologists studying how single molecules organize and move inside living cells. Using new optical tools, his group collects time-series data and extracts the regulatory and organizational principles governing cellular structure and signaling. A former Berkeley physicist, he brings single-molecule biophysics and mechanobiology to questions of cell mechanics and force sensing. His group also pursues broader medical applications, exploring computational and cryptographic techniques to make health data more accessible for disease prevention and public health.

faculty.name: Michael Z. Lin
faculty.title: Professor of Neurobiology and of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Lin engineers molecular tools that let researchers visualize and control biological activity in living cells and animals. His lab has developed bright fluorescent proteins, genetically encoded voltage and biochemical sensors, photoswitchable and protease-based actuators, and luciferase reporters for noninvasive imaging. These optical and chemogenetic tools illuminate neuronal circuit signaling and cellular dynamics, and his group has rewired cancer signaling toward therapeutic outputs. Working across neurobiology and bioengineering, Lin bridges protein engineering, imaging, and neural engineering to reveal how cells compute and communicate.

faculty.name: Alison L. Marsden
faculty.title: Douglass M. and Nola Leishman Professor; Professor of Pediatrics and of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Marsden develops numerical methods for simulating cardiovascular blood flow and applies them to improve treatment of congenital and pediatric heart disease. Her lab combines patient-specific modeling, optimization, and large-scale fluid mechanics to design surgical strategies and medical devices, using virtual surgery to plan operations for children born with heart defects. Bridging computational biomechanics, pediatric cardiology, and bioengineering, she leads open-source cardiovascular simulation software and integrates uncertainty quantification and machine learning to make predictive modeling clinically actionable for patient care.

faculty.name: David B. Camarillo
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Camarillo builds precision measurement technologies to understand and prevent traumatic injury, especially concussion. His lab instruments athletes with wearable inertial sensors and mouthguards to capture how the brain moves during impacts, combining biomechanics, imaging, and modeling to reveal injury mechanisms. Formerly a surgical-robotics engineer at Intuitive Surgical and Hansen Medical, he now extends precision human measurement to the brain, heart, lungs, and reproductive system. His work spans injury biomechanics, medical devices, and sensor engineering aimed at improving clinical diagnosis and prevention.

faculty.name: Vayu Hill-Maini
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Hill-Maini harnesses filamentous fungi to build a more sustainable food system, joining Stanford Bioengineering in 2024. His lab engineers organisms such as Neurospora intermedia to convert agricultural byproducts, including soy pulp and oat-milk waste, into nutritious, umami-rich foods. Trained as a biochemist and Miller Fellow, he blends fermentation, gastronomy, and synthetic biology, having discovered and engineered fungi for food while working with Jay Keasling. His interdisciplinary program treats cooking and metabolic engineering together, aiming to transform waste streams into flavorful, protein-rich ingredients at scale.

faculty.name: Rogelio Hernández-López
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Bioengineering and of Genetics
faculty.summary: Hernández-López engineers immune cells to recognize and destroy tumors with greater precision, working at the interface of synthetic, mechanistic, and systems biology. His lab reprograms molecular circuits so that T cells can count antigen density and decide whether to kill, sparing healthy tissue. A Chan Zuckerberg Biohub investigator, he combines protein and receptor engineering with quantitative modeling to design next-generation cell therapies. His immunoengineering research aims to make cellular therapeutics safer and more selective, translating fundamental signaling insights into programmable treatments for cancer.

faculty.name: Stephen R. Quake
faculty.title: Lee Otterson Professor; Professor of Bioengineering and of Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Quake pioneered microfluidic labs on a chip and used them to transform single-cell biology and genomics. His inventions enabled single-cell gene expression and genome sequencing, the first single-molecule human genome sequence, noninvasive prenatal diagnostics, and blood tests for transplant rejection. His lab helped build whole-organism transcriptomic cell atlases and tools to sequence the immune system. An HHMI investigator working across bioengineering and applied physics, he continues developing measurement technologies at the interface of physics, cell biology, and genomics, and co-leads major biohub efforts.

faculty.name: Michael A. Fischbach
faculty.title: Liu (Liao) Family Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Fischbach studies the human microbiome and how its microbes shape health, aiming ultimately to build therapeutic microbial communities from the ground up. His lab combines genomics and chemistry to discover the small molecules bacteria use to interact with the host, decoding mechanisms of microbiome-host signaling. Director of the Stanford Microbiome Therapies Initiative, he engineers commensal bacteria as programmable drug-delivery and immune-modulating agents. Bridging computational biology, chemical biology, and bioengineering, his work seeks to turn microbiome science into designed, precise, living therapeutics.

faculty.name: Todd P. Coleman
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Coleman designs wearable bioelectronics and flexible sensors that monitor physiology continuously and unobtrusively. His multidisciplinary lab draws on applied probability, physiology, and materials to create skin-like devices that track heart rate, temperature, and neural and autonomic signals, wirelessly streaming data to phones and cloud systems. A Wu Tsai Neurosciences faculty scholar, he applies these tools to neurological and autonomic health, maternal-fetal monitoring, and brain-computer interfaces. His neural-engineering research blends signal processing, bioelectronics, and quantitative neuroscience to bring medical-grade sensing into everyday life.

faculty.name: Sarah C. Heilshorn
faculty.title: Professor of Materials Science & Engineering and, by courtesy, of Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Heilshorn engineers bioinspired materials by integrating protein science with materials engineering, designing substrates that guide cell behavior for regenerative medicine. Her lab creates injectable, tunable hydrogels for stem-cell therapies, 3D bioprinting inks, and ex vivo tissue mimics, with projects targeting spinal cord and blood-vessel regeneration. She also builds microfluidic devices to study directed cell migration. Directing Stanford's Geballe Laboratory for Advanced Materials, she bridges materials science, chemical engineering, and bioengineering to develop programmable biomaterials that support tissue repair and controlled cell delivery.

requirements.english: UCL Level 2 (Good): IELTS Academic 7.0 overall (min 6.5 in each component), or TOEFL iBT 96 overall (24 in reading/writing, 22 in speaking/listening).
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants apply to the cohort programme, not a named supervisor. Year one is coursework plus lab rotations; students then choose a project and supervisory team (from SWC or affiliated Gatsby faculty) within the first months of year two. Note: the standard UCL department MPhil/PhD route is instead direct supervisor match.

funding.note: Fully funded studentships for any nationality: full UCL home/international tuition plus a tax-free stipend starting at GBP 28,400/year (year 1) and a travel budget, for the full four years.

requirements.contact_note: For the rotation-based SWC route, applicants do not pre-arrange or contact a specific supervisor before applying (unlike the standard UCL department route).

faculty.name: Tom Mrsic-Flogel
faculty.title: Professor of Neuroscience; Director, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: Mrsic-Flogel's group studies how activity distributed across many brain regions gives rise to perceptual decisions under uncertainty. Using custom mouse behavioral tasks with two-photon imaging, high-density Neuropixels recordings, and optogenetics, they map how sensory signals are transformed into actions across cortex, basal ganglia, and cerebellum. Their 2024 Nature paper on brain-wide dynamics linking sensation to action during decision-making exemplifies a shift from single-circuit to brain-wide, population-level accounts of neural computation. He directs the Sainsbury Wellcome Centre.

faculty.name: Sonja Hofer
faculty.title: Professor of Neuroscience; Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: The Hofer lab investigates how the brain processes visual information and integrates it with other signals across distributed cortical and thalamic networks. Recent directions include predictive processing and prediction-error computation, subcortical circuits governing the explore-exploit trade-off, and thalamic pathways for flexible fear responses. A 2025 Science paper showed how visual cortex instructs learning to suppress fear responses, with relevance to phobias and PTSD. The group combines imaging, electrophysiology, and circuit manipulation in mice.

faculty.name: Troy Margrie
faculty.title: Professor of Systems Neuroscience; Associate Director, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: Margrie's lab studies how the mammalian brain combines sensory, motor, and vestibular signals to support navigation and flexible behavior, mapping circuits at single-cell resolution with electron microscopy, electrophysiology, viral tracing, imaging, and optogenetics. Recent work shows how visual cortex separates self-generated from external visual motion and identifies layer-6 neurons as major routes for intra- and interhemispheric feedback. The group has also established novel in vivo recordings in fiddler crabs as a tractable navigation model.

faculty.name: Tiago Branco
faculty.title: Professor of Neuroscience and Behaviour; Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: Branco's lab investigates how neural circuits compute instinctive defensive behaviors in mice, including how animals detect threats and select actions such as escape. Their work shows mice rapidly learn optimal escape routes after only minutes of exploration and can suppress innate escape when threats prove harmless, combining hard-wired heuristics with learned strategies. The group uses high-density recordings, two-photon microscopy, viral circuit tracing, and computational modeling to link circuit mechanisms to behavior.

faculty.name: Athena Akrami
faculty.title: Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: The Akrami lab studies how the nervous system learns the statistical structure of the environment and uses memory to make inferences, integrating theory with rodent behavior, imaging, and electrophysiology. A signature line of work uses a parametric working-memory task in rats to explain contraction bias, whereby memories are pulled toward the sensory mean, revealing how prior statistics shape short-term memory. The lab has also contributed to characterizing long-COVID symptomatology across many organ systems.

faculty.name: Marcus Stephenson-Jones
faculty.title: Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: Stephenson-Jones's lab studies how subcortical circuits, particularly the basal ganglia, transform sensory information into goal-directed, motivated behavior. Using rodent behavior with two-photon calcium imaging, fiber photometry, electrophysiology, optogenetics, and intersectional viral labeling, the group has shown that distinct output structures compute different aspects of prediction and outcome. A 2025 paper describes dopaminergic neurons that provide a value-free teaching signal for learning, refining models of reinforcement in the brain.

faculty.name: Andrew Saxe
faculty.title: Professor of Theoretical Neuroscience and Machine Learning; Joint Group Leader, Gatsby Unit & SWC
faculty.summary: Saxe develops mathematical theory for how learning unfolds in biological and artificial neural networks, addressing how internal representations emerge and evolve during training, how prior knowledge shapes learning, and the dynamics of nonlinear and context-dependent networks. His analytic work on learning dynamics in deep networks bridges machine learning and systems neuroscience. Recognition includes a Schmidt Science Polymaths Award and a Human Frontier Science Program grant on the emergence of collective intelligence. He is jointly appointed at Gatsby and SWC.

faculty.name: Maneesh Sahani
faculty.title: Professor of Theoretical Neuroscience and Machine Learning; Director, Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit
faculty.summary: Sahani leads the Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit, developing statistical and machine-learning frameworks for understanding how neural populations represent and compute with uncertainty. Recent work includes methods to infer context-dependent computations from prefrontal-cortex dynamics, showing how learning shapes cortical dynamics to enhance integration of relevant sensory input, and resolving recurrent contributions to neural computation through residual dynamics. His group also studies how the brain may represent and compute with probabilities and latent variables.

faculty.name: John O'Keefe
faculty.title: Professor of Cognitive Neuroscience; Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre
faculty.summary: O'Keefe, who discovered hippocampal place cells and shared the 2014 Nobel Prize, continues to study how the hippocampus and entorhinal cortex support spatial memory and navigation, alongside short-term memory mechanisms in the amygdala. Recent work using the honeycomb maze showed that CA1 place cells form goal-oriented vector fields that converge near goal locations, supporting a vector-based cognitive map for optimal navigation. He authored a 2025 review on how the hippocampal cognitive map supports flexible navigation.

faculty.name: Claudia Clopath
faculty.title: Part-time Group Leader, Sainsbury Wellcome Centre; Professor, Imperial College London
faculty.summary: Clopath is a theoretical and computational neuroscientist whose group builds mathematical models of synaptic plasticity and learning, addressing how neural networks store memories, remain stable, and learn continually without catastrophic forgetting. Her models of spike-timing- and voltage-dependent plasticity connect biophysical rules to network-level function and behavior, and she frequently collaborates with experimental SWC labs to test theoretical predictions. She holds a part-time appointment at SWC alongside her primary group at Imperial College London.

faculty.name: Joel N. Meyer
faculty.title: Sally Kleberg Distinguished Professor; Professor of Environmental Genomics
faculty.summary: Studies the toxicity and genotoxicity of environmental contaminants including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and nanomaterials, with a particular focus on effects on mitochondria and mitochondrial DNA.

faculty.name: Heather M. Stapleton
faculty.title: Ronie-Richele Garcia-Johnson Distinguished Professor; Professor in the Division of Environmental Natural Sciences
faculty.summary: Investigates the sources, fate, transport, transformation, and metabolism of organic contaminants, emphasizing flame retardants and mechanisms of thyroid system disruption.

faculty.name: Nishad Jayasundara
faculty.title: Juli Plant Grainger Assistant Professor of Global Environmental Health; Assistant Professor of Environmental Toxicology and Health
faculty.summary: Uses a One Health framework to study the ecological and human health implications of chemical pollution.

faculty.name: Sven-Eric Jordt
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor of Anesthesiology; Professor of Pharmacology and Cancer Biology
faculty.summary: Investigates the mechanisms of environmental chemical irritation and chemical sensing and their contributions to inflammation, asthma, and lung injury.

faculty.name: Theodore Slotkin
faculty.title: Professor of Pharmacology and Cancer Biology
faculty.summary: Studies the effects of environmental toxicants including insecticides and nanoparticles, particularly on nervous-system development.

faculty.name: Edward D. Levin
faculty.title: Professor in Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences; Professor of Psychology and Neuroscience
faculty.summary: Studies toxicological influences on learning and memory, utilizing both rodent and zebrafish models.

faculty.name: Donald P. McDonnell
faculty.title: Glaxo-Wellcome Distinguished Professor of Molecular Cancer Biology; Professor of Pharmacology and Cancer Biology
faculty.summary: Studies the molecular mechanisms through which both therapeutic agents and endocrine disrupters modulate cell function.

faculty.name: Christopher M. Counter
faculty.title: George Barth Geller Distinguished Professor of Pharmacology; Professor of Pharmacology and Cancer Biology
faculty.summary: Examines how environmental carcinogens induce specific cancers using mouse models and sequencing approaches.

faculty.name: Laurie H. Sanders
faculty.title: Associate Professor in Neurology; Associate Professor of Pathology
faculty.summary: Investigates environmental influences on mitochondrial DNA damage in neurological disorders.

faculty.name: Samira Musah
faculty.title: Assistant Professor in the Department of Biomedical Engineering; Assistant Professor in Medicine
faculty.summary: Develops stem cell and organ-on-chip systems to model toxicity and disease mechanisms.

faculty.name: Robert M. Tighe
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Medicine; Associate Professor in the Division of Environmental Natural Sciences
faculty.summary: Identifies susceptibility factors relevant to host responses to environmental pollutants, studying mechanisms of air pollution exposure and lung injury and repair.

faculty.name: Kate Hoffman
faculty.title: Elizabeth Brooks Reid and Whitelaw Reid Associate Research Professor of Environmental Natural Sciences
faculty.summary: Investigates environmental contaminant exposure pathways, particularly among children.

faculty.name: Junfeng (Jim) Zhang
faculty.title: Professor of Global and Environmental Health
faculty.summary: Develops novel biomarkers of exposure and adverse health effects related to air pollution and studies the co-benefits of pollution-reduction interventions.

faculty.name: Christine K. Payne
faculty.title: Professor in the Thomas Lord Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science
faculty.summary: Investigates the cellular response to nanoparticles present in the environment.

faculty.name: Michael H. Bergin
faculty.title: Professor in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering
faculty.summary: Investigates the influence of air pollution, particularly PM2.5, on both climate and human health.

faculty.name: Yan Lin
faculty.title: Assistant Research Professor in the Division of Environmental Natural Sciences
faculty.summary: Examines the role of lipid metabolism in cardiopulmonary diseases arising from air pollution exposure.

faculty.name: Mercedes Bravo
faculty.title: Assistant Research Professor of Global Health; Assistant Research Professor in the Division of Environmental Natural Sciences
faculty.summary: Studies connections between social and environmental exposures and health disparities, focusing on air pollution and environmental justice.

faculty.name: AnnMarie Walton
faculty.title: Dorothy L. Powell Term Chair of Nursing; Associate Professor in the School of Nursing
faculty.summary: Studies occupational exposure to carcinogens, particularly pesticide exposure among farmworkers.

faculty.name: Dina M. Fonseca
faculty.title: Professor, Chair of the Department of Entomology, Director of the Center for Vector Biology
faculty.summary: Fonseca examines how invasive mosquito populations differ in disease-transmission capacity across space and time, affecting epidemiological risk. She develops mosquito control strategies through extension partnerships and citizen-science initiatives that use environmental DNA to detect and manage invasive species.

faculty.name: Alvaro Toledo
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Toledo investigates ticks and tick-borne pathogens, particularly Borrelia burgdorferi (the Lyme disease agent). His work examines how cholesterol and lipid rafts function in bacterial sensing and adaptation, along with the role of tick microbiomes in pathogen transmission.

faculty.name: Dana C. Price
faculty.title: Associate Research Professor
faculty.summary: Price's lab conducts functional genomic analyses of vector arthropods and their associated microorganisms to identify and surveil emerging zoonotic infectious diseases. Current work includes non-destructive pathogen sampling, reverse-vaccine approaches for invasive ticks, and spatiotemporal studies of tick-borne and arbovirus disease dynamics.

faculty.name: Chloe Hawkings
faculty.title: Assistant Professor, Undergraduate Program Director
faculty.summary: Hawkings investigates the physiological mechanisms that underlie social insect behavior. Her work uses gene expression analysis to examine how insects adapt ecologically and make behavioral decisions across different life stages and castes.

faculty.name: Lena B. Brattsten
faculty.title: Research Professor
faculty.summary: Brattsten investigates biochemical resistance mechanisms in insects exposed to toxic chemicals, focusing on detoxification enzymes such as cytochrome P450s and general esterases. Her work examines physiological, behavioral, and phenological resistance mechanisms alongside the molecular genetics of insect defense systems.

faculty.name: Anne L. Nielsen
faculty.title: Associate Extension Specialist in Entomology, Graduate Program Director
faculty.summary: Nielsen develops phenological models for tree fruit and small fruit pests, integrating insect physiology and behavior into sustainable management programs. Her work emphasizes invasive species, particularly the brown marmorated stink bug (Halyomorpha halys), along with monitoring, biological control, and IPM.

faculty.name: Cesar Rodriguez-Saona
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Entomology
faculty.summary: Rodriguez-Saona develops and implements cost-effective, reduced-risk integrated pest management practices for blueberries and cranberries. His work integrates chemical, behavioral, and biological control methods with an understanding of pest ecology and natural enemies.

faculty.name: Albrecht Koppenhofer
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Entomology
faculty.summary: Koppenhofer develops ecologically based integrated pest management for turfgrass systems with an emphasis on sustainability and biological control. His research focuses on conservation of natural enemies and interactions among chemical, biological, and cultural control components.

faculty.name: George C. Hamilton
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Pest Management
faculty.summary: Hamilton focuses on implementing integrated pest management programs and insect biocontrol, with current emphasis on conservation biological control for ornamental and vegetable pests. He also works on managing invasive insects including brown marmorated stink bug, spotted lanternfly, and emerald ash borer.

faculty.name: Changlu Wang
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Entomology
faculty.summary: Wang develops urban pest management technologies by studying the biology, behavior, and ecology of pests such as cockroaches, bed bugs, ants, and termites. His extension program delivers research-based urban pest management technologies to pest management professionals and the public.

faculty.name: Andrea Gallavotti
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: His lab investigates the molecular mechanisms regulating plant architecture using maize as a model system, including how the hormone auxin regulates meristem formation and transcriptional repression in development.

faculty.name: Mark Zander
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: His work spans plant (epi)genomics, plant immunity, hormone signaling, and transcriptional regulation, focused on understanding the molecular mechanisms governing plant defense and developmental regulation.

faculty.name: Eric Lam
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: He studies mechanisms controlling programmed cell death and stress tolerance in plants and the regulation of gene expression via chromatin organization, with current work developing duckweed-based aquatic agriculture including a reference genome for Spirodela polyrhiza.

faculty.name: Pal Maliga
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: He specializes in plastid molecular biology within the broader areas of molecular biology, biotechnology, and plant genetic improvement.

faculty.name: Nilgun Tumer
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor, Director of SEBS Core Facility
faculty.summary: Her laboratory investigates the basic mechanism of toxicity of ribosome-inactivating proteins such as ricin, Shiga toxins, and trichothecene mycotoxins, using yeast models and fragment-based inhibitor discovery.

faculty.name: Ning Zhang
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: She investigates the taxonomy, biodiversity, and genomics of Magnaporthales, an ascomycete fungal order containing several important plant pathogens, and develops molecular methods for rapid diagnosis of fungal pathogens.

faculty.name: James F. White, Jr.
faculty.title: Professor of Plant Pathology
faculty.summary: He specializes in symbiosis research, particularly endophytic microbes, investigating the diversity of endophytic microorganisms and their various effects on host plants.

faculty.name: Donald Y. Kobayashi
faculty.title: Professor, Department Chair
faculty.summary: His laboratory focuses on characterizing bacterial plant pathogens and the diseases they cause, especially in the greater northeast region of the US.

faculty.name: Peter Oudemans
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: He specializes in plant pathology of blueberry and cranberry, studying pathogenic fungi affecting these berry crops and using geospatial (GIS) technologies for disease management.

faculty.name: Michael Lawton
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: He studies disease resistance mechanisms and pathogen interactions, with a focus on fungal and bacterial pathogens, Fusarium head blight, toxins, and programmed cell death using functional genomics approaches.

faculty.name: C. Andrew Wyenandt
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Vegetable Pathology
faculty.summary: His lab investigates fungicide programs for managing diseases such as phytophthora blight and anthracnose in peppers and cucurbits, evaluates control of basil downy mildew, and develops fungicide resistance management strategies.

faculty.name: Stacy Bonos
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: She develops improved turfgrass species with enhanced disease resistance and stress tolerance, and breeds switchgrass for biomass yield and bioenergy applications.

faculty.name: Gina Sideli
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Her lab focuses on classical and molecular breeding of cranberry and blueberry, integrating genomics and phenomics to understand trait genetics and implement marker-assisted or genomic selection for crop improvement.

faculty.name: Thomas J. Molnar
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: His work concentrates on the genetic improvement of hazelnuts (Corylus) and large-bracted dogwoods, including developing and characterizing genetic resistance to eastern filbert blight, the primary limiting factor of hazelnut production in eastern North America.

faculty.name: Joseph Goffreda
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: His research area, per the department directory, is the breeding of tree fruit crops including peach, nectarine, apple, and apricot.

faculty.name: Rong Di
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Her lab uses the C. elegans model to study anti-diabetic, anti-obesity, anti-Alzheimer's, anti-inflammatory, and anti-carcinogenic effects of plant extracts, and employs genetic engineering and CRISPR to develop disease-resistant and nutrition-enhanced plants.

faculty.name: Ilya Raskin
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: His research has spanned plant growth regulation, plant immunity to disease, and phytoremediation, and in the last decade has focused on plant-derived functional foods and medicines alongside international bioexploration and conservation.

faculty.name: James E. Simon
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.sub_field: New crop development and natural products
faculty.summary: He specializes in new crop development, domestication of high-value crops, non-timber forest species, and sustainable development of indigenous plant resources.

faculty.name: Lena Struwe
faculty.title: Professor and Director of Chrysler Herbarium
faculty.summary: She studies plant evolutionary biology and systematics (particularly gentians and urban weeds) alongside modern ethnobotany, with over 25 years of experience in plant diversity and botanical nomenclature across multiple continents.

faculty.name: Bingru Huang
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Her expertise is in turfgrass stress physiology, biochemistry, and molecular biology.

faculty.name: Chi-Tang Ho
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Professor Ho's research concentrates on flavor chemistry and the bioactivities of natural products, and he has authored more than 700 scholarly papers during his tenure at Rutgers.

faculty.name: Karen M. Schaich
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Schaich uses EPR spectroscopy to study free radical intermediates and their reactions, focusing on the mechanisms of lipid oxidation across contexts ranging from food preservation to cellular pathology.

faculty.name: Qingrong Huang
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.sub_field: Food Chemistry / Food Colloids and Nanotechnology
faculty.summary: Dr. Huang's work focuses on the assembly and applications of food colloids and biopolymers, including nano- and micro-encapsulation technologies for delivering active food ingredients.

faculty.name: Michael Chikindas
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Chikindas investigates natural-derived antimicrobials and probiotics for use in human health, personal care, and agricultural applications, and has authored over 100 papers and multiple book chapters on these topics.

faculty.name: Karl R. Matthews
faculty.title: Professor, Graduate Program Director
faculty.summary: Dr. Matthews investigates microbial safety in food production and processing, focusing on antibiotic-resistant bacteria in vegetable crops and antimicrobial treatments using light and plant-derived compounds to control foodborne pathogens.

faculty.name: Donald W. Schaffner
faculty.title: Department Chair, Distinguished Professor, Extension Specialist
faculty.sub_field: Food Microbiology / Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment
faculty.summary: Dr. Schaffner's work focuses on quantitative microbial risk assessment, predictive food microbiology, and handwashing and cross-contamination, with expertise extending to pandemic-related microbial transmission risks.

faculty.name: George M. Carman
faculty.title: Board of Governors Professor, Distinguished Professor; Founding Director, Rutgers Center for Lipid Research
faculty.summary: Dr. Carman's laboratory has contributed to understanding lipid synthesis and regulation through the isolation and characterization of key genes and their encoded enzymes, with research continuously supported by NIH grants since 1980.

faculty.name: Loredana Quadro
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Quadro is a Professor of Food Science and member of the Rutgers Center for Lipid Research whose research addresses the genetics and metabolism of fat-soluble vitamins and nutrient metabolism.

faculty.name: Diana E. Roopchand
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.sub_field: Nutritional Sciences / Gut Microbiota and Dietary Phytochemicals
faculty.summary: Dr. Roopchand investigates how plant phytochemicals such as dietary polyphenols interact with gut microbiota and the host to explain health benefits, and also explores cannabidiol's potential therapeutic applications for menopausal women through changes in estrogen metabolism.

faculty.name: Nuanyi Liang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Liang applies advanced analytical techniques to investigate lipid metabolites and oxylipins and their connections to chronic disease prevention, with applications in sustainable food innovation and food upcycling.

faculty.name: Tianming Yao
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Yao investigates how the structure of dietary fibers and complex polysaccharides influences gut microbiota composition and function, and how these interactions promote beneficial metabolic outcomes and microbial diversity.

faculty.name: Beverly J. Tepper
faculty.title: Professor, Graduate Program Director
faculty.summary: Dr. Tepper investigates how genetic variation in taste perception influences food preferences, dietary choices, and body weight, using genetic markers such as PROP and the oral fat sensor CD36, and studies salivary proteins in taste perception and oral health.

faculty.name: Mukund V. Karwe
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Karwe's research focuses on advanced food processing technologies, including high pressure processing, cold plasma processing, and extrusion methods for food applications.

faculty.name: Paul Takhistov
faculty.title: Associate Professor, Master of Business and Science Program Director
faculty.summary: Dr. Takhistov's group develops innovative food products using formulation engineering principles, emphasizing shelf-life extension, supply-chain stability, and contamination prevention through intelligent packaging, smart sensing, and non-thermal processing technologies.

faculty.name: Kit L. Yam
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Yam's research addresses food packaging, including controlled and modified atmosphere packaging and packaging development, as listed in the department faculty directory.

faculty.name: Carol A. Bagnell
faculty.title: Professor and Chair, Department of Animal Sciences
faculty.summary: Dr. Bagnell's lab studies how maternal factors transmitted through nursing (the 'lactocrine' mechanism) influence neonatal development, examining immediate and long-term effects of milk-borne signals on reproductive health and fertility.

faculty.name: Nicholas T. Bello
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.sub_field: Neuroscience of feeding behavior and obesity
faculty.summary: Dr. Bello's lab investigates mechanisms by which dietary supplements prevent metabolic consequences of obesity in rodent models, and how dysregulation of brain norepinephrine pathways contributes to aberrant eating behaviors and loss-of-control episodes in binge eating.

faculty.name: Wendie S. Cohick
faculty.title: Vice Provost for Research and Professor
faculty.sub_field: Endocrine physiology and mammary gland biology
faculty.summary: Dr. Cohick's lab examines how the insulin-like growth factor system regulates mammary gland physiology and breast cancer development, including stromal-epithelial interactions and how prenatal alcohol exposure influences lifetime breast cancer susceptibility.

faculty.name: Meli'sa S. Crawford
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.sub_field: Environmental physiology and toxicology of metabolic disease
faculty.summary: Dr. Crawford investigates how environmental pollutants from air and food sources contribute to metabolic diseases and gastrointestinal disorders through altered gut bacteria, intestinal inflammation, and organ crosstalk, using animal models to trace pollutant pathways.

faculty.name: Kenneth H. McKeever
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.summary: Professor McKeever's research focuses on equine physiology and performance, with expertise in cardiovascular responses to exercise, bridging exercise science and animal agriculture at Rutgers' Equine Science Center.

faculty.name: Troy A. Roepke
faculty.title: Professor and Associate Dean of Faculty Development and Workplace Culture (SEBS)
faculty.summary: Dr. Roepke investigates how environmental stresses and hormones affect hypothalamic physiological functions, with focus on novel membrane-initiated estrogen signaling pathways and the long-term effects of low-dose endocrine disruptor exposure on offspring.

faculty.name: Dipak K. Sarkar
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor and Director, Endocrine Program
faculty.summary: Dr. Sarkar investigates how prenatal alcohol exposure disrupts neuroimmune signaling in the hypothalamus through epigenetic pathways, causing stress dysregulation, metabolic dysfunction, immune deficiency, and increased cancer susceptibility.

faculty.name: Igor Shmarakov
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Shmarakov studies how retinoids and retinoid-interacting proteins regulate physiological functions across hepatic, adipose, pulmonary, and intestinal cells, examining disrupted retinoid signaling in lung injury and metabolic diseases.

faculty.name: Elizabeth Snyder
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.sub_field: Male reproductive biology and germ cell RNA biology
faculty.summary: Dr. Snyder's lab studies male germ cell RNA biology to improve reproductive health in animals and humans, focusing on novel RNA expression and nucleotide-level RNA modifications using computational, molecular, and genetic approaches.

faculty.name: Mehmet Uzumcu
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.summary: Dr. Uzumcu investigates how environmental factors and endocrine-disrupting chemicals affect the female reproductive system's long-term health, examining developmental effects of pesticides and plasticizers on ovarian follicular composition and gene expression following fetal and neonatal exposures.

faculty.name: Tess Stahl
faculty.title: Assistant Extension Specialist in Animal Sciences
faculty.summary: Dr. Stahl studies prepartum nutrition changes in beef cows, sheep, and goats and their effects on offspring development, colostrum quantity and quality, and the use of byproduct feeds in livestock diets.

faculty.name: Carey A. Williams
faculty.title: Extension Specialist in Equine Management
faculty.summary: Dr. Williams investigates how nutrition impacts equine athletic performance, focusing on reducing oxidative stress and inflammation with antioxidant supplements, and examining grazing system effects on equine health and gut microbial communities.

faculty.name: Tracy G. Anthony
faculty.title: Professor of Nutritional Sciences (Graduate Program Co-Director)
faculty.sub_field: Amino acid sensing and metabolic signaling
faculty.summary: Anthony's lab studies how the body senses nutrients and converts that information into decisions about growth, stress resistance, and survival, using mouse models, cell culture, and omics approaches to examine nutrient-sensing pathways during stress and aging.

faculty.name: Paul Breslin
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.sub_field: Genetics of human oral perception and nutrient intake
faculty.summary: Breslin investigates the genetic basis of human oral perception and its impact on nutrient intake, studying taste discrimination, chemesthesis, and flavor using both human research and parallel genetic studies in Drosophila.

faculty.name: Carol Byrd-Bredbenner
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor of Nutritional Sciences (Director, Graduate Program in Nutritional Sciences)
faculty.summary: Her work uses a socioecological approach to examine intrapersonal, interpersonal, and environmental factors affecting dietary choices, developing and validating theory-driven nutrition education interventions that promote healthier lifestyles.

faculty.name: Joseph L. Dixon
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Dixon's laboratory investigates how abnormal blood lipids contribute to coronary artery disease, focusing on apoB-lipoprotein secretion from liver cells and altered lipoprotein composition in diabetes that may accelerate atherosclerosis.

faculty.name: Mackenzie Ferrante
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.sub_field: Behavioral nutrition and children's eating behaviors
faculty.summary: Ferrante's research investigates the predictors of young children's eating behaviors early in life and develops environmental and behavioral interventions across restaurants, grocery stores, and homes to foster healthy dietary habits and prevent obesity.

faculty.name: Nurgül Fitzgerald
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Nutritional Sciences / Extension Specialist
faculty.sub_field: Type 2 diabetes prevention and health disparities
faculty.summary: Fitzgerald studies prevention of type 2 diabetes risk factors among underserved populations, examining individual, family, organizational, community, and policy-level determinants and integrating food systems analysis with lifestyle intervention research.

faculty.name: Christy Gliniak
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Gliniak's lab studies adipose tissue biology and interorgan crosstalk during obesity and inflammatory bowel disease, with a focus on lipid regulation of cellular stress and the role of mesenteric visceral fat in metabolic disease.

faculty.name: Daniel J. Hoffman
faculty.title: Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Hoffman studies how poor early-life nutrition affects long-term health, investigating metabolic adaptations in growth-stunted children and how households in transitional countries respond to economic crises through changes in food purchasing and diet.

faculty.name: Joshua W. Miller
faculty.title: Professor and Chair of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Miller investigates how B vitamins and vitamin D influence cognitive decline and neurodegenerative disease in aging populations, examining relationships among homocysteine, vitamin deficiencies, Alzheimer's disease, and hypertension.

faculty.name: Virginia Quick
faculty.title: Associate Teaching Professor of Nutritional Sciences (Director, Didactic Program in Dietetics)
faculty.summary: Quick's research examines the interplay of intrapersonal, interpersonal, and socio-environmental factors affecting health behaviors using a socioecological model, developing evidence-based interventions for obesity and disordered eating across the lifespan.

faculty.name: Harini Sampath
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Sampath's lab investigates cellular alterations during the development of metabolic diseases such as fatty liver, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes, using transgenic mouse models and cell culture to examine lipid metabolism regulation and DNA repair pathways.

faculty.name: Sue A. Shapses
faculty.title: Professor of Nutritional Sciences (Director, Human Nutrition, Exercise and Metabolism Center)
faculty.sub_field: Endocrine regulation of obesity and bone metabolism
faculty.summary: Shapses examines mechanisms that regulate organs affected during caloric restriction and how nutrients such as protein, monounsaturated fatty acids, and vitamin D influence bone metabolism and metabolic health, particularly in aging populations.

faculty.name: Judith Storch
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor of Nutritional Sciences
faculty.summary: Storch investigates how lipids are transported and targeted within cells, examining fatty acid-binding proteins and the NPC2 cholesterol-trafficking protein using biochemical, biophysical, cellular, and whole-animal approaches relevant to obesity, cardiovascular, and lipid-storage diseases.

faculty.name: Malcolm Watford
faculty.title: Professor of Nutritional Sciences (Undergraduate Program Director)
faculty.summary: Watford's laboratory studies the role of amino acids, notably glutamine and glutamate, as substrates for gluconeogenesis and urea synthesis, examining tissue-specific glutamine metabolism and the regulation of glutaminase and glutamine synthetase across species.
